title: The Übermensch Is Not a Superman: Nietzsche's Most Misread Idea

source.original: Thus Spoke Zarathustra — Friedrich Nietzsche / Atlas Shrugged — Ayn Rand

body_html:
The Übermensch might be the most catastrophically misunderstood concept in philosophy. Thanks to Nazi appropriation and bad translations ("Superman"), most people picture a blond demigod dominating lesser humans. Nietzsche would be disgusted. The Übermensch isn't about domination. It's about self-overcoming.

The German is precise: Über (over, beyond) + Mensch (human). Not super-man but beyond-human — someone who has overcome the human tendency toward comfort, conformity, and the herd. The Übermensch doesn't rule others. He rules himself. He creates his own values instead of inheriting them. He says yes to life — all of it, including suffering — instead of seeking escape in religion, ideology, or the Last Man's invented happiness.

Rand understood this better than most Nietzsche scholars. Her heroes aren't strongmen. Howard Roark doesn't want to dominate architecture — he wants to build his buildings. Hank Rearden doesn't want to crush competitors — he wants to pour his metal. Dagny Taggart doesn't want power over the railroad — she wants to run it at its potential. The Randian hero, like the Übermensch, is competing with one person: yesterday's version of themselves.

This is where the Nazis got it exactly backwards. They read "beyond-human" as "master race" — a biological category. Nietzsche was talking about a psychological achievement. The Übermensch can come from any blood, any nation, any class. The entire point of the Dagny-James sibling device is this: same DNA, opposite outcomes. The beyond-human isn't born. It's chosen, moment by moment, in every decision to create rather than resent, to build rather than obstruct, to see clearly rather than blink.

The self-overcoming aspect is critical and usually ignored. Nietzsche didn't say the Übermensch is someone without weaknesses. He said it's someone who uses their weaknesses as raw material for growth. Eternal recurrence — the thought experiment where you must live your exact life infinitely — is the test: can you say yes to every failure, every humiliation, every loss, because they were necessary steps in becoming who you are? The Last Man says "I wish things were different." The Übermensch says "amor fati" — love of fate.

In practice, the Übermensch isn't a destination. It's a direction. Nietzsche called the human "a rope between animal and Übermensch — a rope over an abyss." The point isn't to arrive. It's to keep crossing. The moment you stop — the moment you declare yourself finished, satisfied, comfortable — you've started blinking. And the Last Man smiles, because he just gained another member.
